topic: Two friends plan separate birthday parties on the same day
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled napkin lay on the table, a testament to the sheer audacity of the plan. "Are you serious?" I finally managed, my voice tight enough to snap. Liam just shrugged, fiddling with the string of fairy lights. He had the nerve to suggest we both throw separate celebrations for my birthday on the very same afternoon. I felt a hot flush rising up my neck, the air in the room suddenly feeling too thick to breathe. Every muscle in my jaw was clenched so tightly I could taste copper. "It's just... different," he mumbled, avoiding my gaze. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of wood against the floor sounding like a gunshot in the silence. "Different how, Liam? Because this feels like a deliberate slight." I turned sharply and walked towards the door, the frustration a heavy weight pressing down on my chest.

[story 2]

My hands were shaking as I reread the hastily scribbled itinerary. The ink looked smeared, mirroring the mess in my head. "This is ridiculous," I muttered under my breath, slamming the planner shut. Sarah looked up, her expression one of placid confusion. "What's wrong?" she asked, tilting her head. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the tremor in my hands. "The timing," I said, the words coming out clipped and sharp. "You want me to spend my birthday celebrating alone? After everything we’ve been through? It just feels… dismissive." I paced the small living room, the floorboards protesting under my quick steps. I could feel the heat radiating off my skin, a restless energy demanding an outlet, a release from this simmering feeling that wouldn't dissipate.

[story 3]

I stared at the calendar, the date circled aggressively in red marker. The sheer misalignment of the situation made my stomach churn. "So, we're splitting the celebration?" I asked, my tone dangerously level. There was no humor in the question, just a brittle edge to it. Mark just looked down at his shoes, fiddling with the laces repeatedly. I felt a surge of hot anger that made my palms sweat. "Yes, Mark. We are splitting it. Because clearly, one of us needs a day where they aren't burdened by the other's presence." I leaned against the counter, crossing my arms tightly over my chest. The silence stretched, thick with unspoken accusations, and I waited, daring him to respond to the palpable tension radiating off me.
